purpose: Define the hygiene classification pass used to identify unnecessary surfaces in an independent service and flag archive or delete-review candidates without central runtime ownership.

scan_axes.question: Is this file, folder, or flow no longer used by the current runtime path?
Does this surface duplicate another active surface without providing a distinct operational role?
Does this surface still represent an old governance path that is no longer canonical?
Can this surface be archived without breaking the live runtime or the current governance kernel?
Does this surface indicate that the service root is drifting away from the declared profile?
Has this surface grown beyond what the current runtime profile can justify?

status_levels.meaning: No diet scan has been completed yet.
A first-pass classification scan is complete, but no cleanup move is authorized yet.
A deeper scan has confirmed archive-safe candidates and delete-review candidates.
The service requires cleanup before release readiness can be granted.
A diet pass was attempted but required evidence, rollback notes, or receipts are missing.

hard_rules: Central governance may classify delete-review candidates but actual deletion remains service-local only.
Delete-review candidates must never include protected paths or required kernel paths.
Archive and delete review both require a receipt, a risk note, and a rollback note before any local action executes.
The diet pass must never classify required DTP files or active temporary-link kit files as archive or delete candidates.
Classification alone does not authorize deletion; cleanup execution remains service-local and receipt-gated.
Safe fixes may create missing required paths and rewrite monitoring artifacts, but they may not delete runtime surfaces.
Archive mode may move only archive candidates into a local archive path and must emit a cleanup action receipt.
Cleanup review mode may stage delete-review candidates for human approval, but it must not delete them automatically.
